subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

reviews.author: Puranjay Mohan
reviews.summary: The reviewer noted that panic_smp_self_stop() passes a NULL pointer for 'regs', leading to a synchronous abort in elf_core_copy_regs(). They suggested either materializing the intent of capturing the current context by calling crash_setup_regs() or skipping the save when regs is NULL.

reviews.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged a NULL dereference bug introduced by folding ipi_cpu_crash_stop() and promised to fix it in version 4 of the patch.

Author acknowledged that the self-stopping CPU does not save registers, agreed to drop 'NULL means capture current' wording from comment and restored CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE check around crash_save_cpu() call.
reviews.raw_body:
Good catch, you're right \u2014 that's a real NULL deref, and a regression
from folding ipi_cpu_crash_stop() in (the old code only ran on the IPI
path, which always has regs).

Will be fixed in v4.

---

I went with skipping it. No architecture saves registers for the
self-stopping CPU -- the generic, arm and powerpc panic_smp_self_stop()
all just mark offline and spin, and arm64 did the same via
local_cpu_stop() before this series. crash_save_cpu() is only ever fed
the interrupted context, from the crash shootdown or the crashing CPU
itself. So:

	if (crash && regs)
		crash_save_cpu(regs, cpu);

and I dropped the "NULL means capture current" wording from the comment
rather than make it true.

While fixing this I noticed the same block broke the CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE=n
build: the unification replaced the original ipi_cpu_crash_stop()'s
#ifdef CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE with an IS_ENABLED() check, but <linux/kexec.h>
only declares crash_save_cpu() under CONFIG_KEXEC_CORE (and pulling in
<linux/crash_core.h> directly collides with kexec.h's own stubs), so the
dead-but-compiled call was an implicit-declaration error. Restored the
#ifdef around the crash_save_cpu() call.

Thanks for the review!

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

2026-06-15.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPUs that are wedged and ignoring interrupts. When a CPU is wedged, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 on the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF. This allows the CPU to be stopped even if it's ignoring interrupts. The patch also folds stop bookkeeping into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop().

reviews.author: Doug Anderson
reviews.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson noted that the patch does not handle the case where SDEI is disabled, and requested that a check be added to ensure that sdei_nmi_active() returns true before attempting to signal an SDEI NMI.

Reviewer suggested using kernel-doc format for function comments, noting that no other functions in the file use this style and wondering why it wasn't adopted here

Reviewer Doug Anderson pointed out that the patch is missing an smp_rmb() barrier to match the existing smp_wmb(), which could lead to a data race.
sentiment_signals: requested additional check for SDEI availability
reviews.raw_body:
Hi,

On Sun, Jun 14, 2026 at 7:36 PM Kiryl Shutsemau <kirill@shutemov.name> wrote:

---

Nice to have all the details in the function comment. Any reason why
you didn't use kernel-doc format? Nothing else in this file does, I
guess, but it doesn't seem like it would be a problem to start the
trend... ;-)

---

Don't you need a smp_rmb() before that, to match with the smp_wmb()?

-Doug

2026-06-16.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPUs that are wedged and ignoring interrupts. When a CPU is wedged, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 on the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF. This allows the CPU to be stopped even if it's ignoring interrupts. The patch also folds stop bookkeeping into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop().
